title: Japanese name suffixes
wikitext:
When watching Neon Genesis Evangelion in the original Japanese, you may hear the following suffixes attached to personal names; these are known as '''honorifics'''. The Japanese used in anime often takes liberty with forms of personal address, but in real-life Japan, one uses "-san" as the default honorific for someone close to one's own station, and one would ''never'' address someone by only their first name. Calling a person by their first name without honorific indicates a very close relationship, such as living together as a married couple.

====''-san'' (さん)====
Equivalent to Mr or Ms/Mrs in Japanese. Used for people you don't know real well or people in higher authority than you.

====''-kun'' (君)====
Denotes more of a close relationship. Longtime friends may refer to each other as this. Usually used for boys, but can be used for girls as well.  ''(Unsure if this would imply the female being masculine or not though)''

====''-chan'' (ちゃん)====
Denotes more of a close relationship. Longtime friends may refer to each other as this. Usually used for girls, but can be used for boys as well, though it could make them seem effeminate (though such things don't necessarily have the weak connotation it does in English). 

====''-sama'' (様)====
Equivalent of Lord or Lady. Denotes people in very high authority, such as royalty and the like.

==Category 2==
====''-sensei'' (先生)====
Can be used for teachers, doctors, etc.

====''-sen/mpai'' (先輩)====
"Senior", as opposed to ''kouhai'', which means "Junior".

==Category 3: Professional Titles==
In alphabetical order. (Missing a few...)

====''-fukushirei'' (副指令)====

"Sub/Vice/Deputy/Etc.-Commander".

====''-hakase'' (博士)====

"Doctor" (Ph.D.).

====''-ichii'' (一尉)====

"Major".

====''-kyouju'' (教授)====

"Professor".

====''-sansa'' (三佐)====

"Captain". 

====''-shirei'' (司令)====

"Commander".

==References==
'''If you put anything from Wikipedia here, you are already dead.'''

html:
When watching Neon Genesis Evangelion in the original Japanese, you may hear the following suffixes attached to personal names; these are known as honorifics. The Japanese used in anime often takes liberty with forms of personal address, but in real-life Japan, one uses "-san" as the default honorific for someone close to one's own station, and one would never address someone by only their first name. Calling a person by their first name without honorific indicates a very close relationship, such as living together as a married couple.

Contents

  • 1 -san (さん)
  • 2 -kun (君)
  • 3 -chan (ちゃん)
  • 4 -sama (様)
  • 5 Category 2
    • 5.1 -sensei (先生)
    • 5.2 -sen/mpai (先輩)
  • 6 Category 3: Professional Titles
    • 6.1 -fukushirei (副指令)
    • 6.2 -hakase (博士)
    • 6.3 -ichii (一尉)
    • 6.4 -kyouju (教授)
    • 6.5 -sansa (三佐)
    • 6.6 -shirei (司令)
  • 7 References

-san (さん)[edit]

Equivalent to Mr or Ms/Mrs in Japanese. Used for people you don't know real well or people in higher authority than you.

-kun (君)[edit]

Denotes more of a close relationship. Longtime friends may refer to each other as this. Usually used for boys, but can be used for girls as well. (Unsure if this would imply the female being masculine or not though)

-chan (ちゃん)[edit]

Denotes more of a close relationship. Longtime friends may refer to each other as this. Usually used for girls, but can be used for boys as well, though it could make them seem effeminate (though such things don't necessarily have the weak connotation it does in English).

-sama (様)[edit]

Equivalent of Lord or Lady. Denotes people in very high authority, such as royalty and the like.

Category 2[edit]

-sensei (先生)[edit]

Can be used for teachers, doctors, etc.

-sen/mpai (先輩)[edit]

"Senior", as opposed to kouhai, which means "Junior".

Category 3: Professional Titles[edit]

In alphabetical order. (Missing a few...)

-fukushirei (副指令)[edit]

"Sub/Vice/Deputy/Etc.-Commander".

-hakase (博士)[edit]

"Doctor" (Ph.D.).

-ichii (一尉)[edit]

"Major".

-kyouju (教授)[edit]

"Professor".

-sansa (三佐)[edit]

"Captain".

-shirei (司令)[edit]

"Commander".
